source.usage_note: Original composition in CAT register for CAT OS; the knowledge-problem tradition it discusses is long-standing in economics, but no specific text is adapted. Personal study only.

meta.reviewer_notes: Authored during Milestone 2 build; self-scored against Pipeline §6 rubric. Owner review pending. Mentor layer added in Reading Mentor milestone (v2); reading-habit + misunderstanding layer added in Reading Experience milestone (v3). Owner review pending for all layers.

paragraphs.text: The deepest defence of market prices has never been that they are fair, nor even that they are efficient in the accountant's sense. It is that they solve a problem no committee can: the problem of knowledge. The information relevant to any economic decision — that a mine has flooded, that a harvest has failed, that a new alloy makes an old process cheap — exists nowhere as a whole. It is scattered across millions of minds, much of it tacit, fleeting, and known only to the person on the spot. Any authority that wished to plan production would first have to gather this dispersed knowledge, and by the time it had been gathered, reported, and reconciled, much of it would already be false.

A price performs the gathering without anyone intending it. When tin becomes scarce — never mind why — its price rises, and every user of tin receives the same terse instruction: economize. The typist need not know about the flooded mine; the engineer need not know that a distant industry has found a new use for the metal. Each responds to the number alone, and the sum of their small adjustments accomplishes what a planner armed with complete files could scarcely match. The marvel is the parsimony of it: a single figure carries the aggregate consequence of countless facts, while discarding the facts themselves.

That discarding, however, is also the system's confession. A price transmits how much of a thing to use, not why; it carries the conclusion of society's dispersed knowledge and none of the reasoning. Where the 'why' matters — where a shortage reflects a cartel rather than a flood, or where the costs of an activity fall on people who never transact in it — the terse instruction misleads or falls silent. Pollution has no invoice; the price of coal has historically said little about the lungs it darkens.

The mature conclusion is therefore neither worship nor dismissal. Prices are an extraordinary instrument for coordinating action under dispersed knowledge, and a poor instrument for registering values that never pass through a transaction. The interesting question about any proposed intervention is not whether it 'interferes with the market,' but whether it corrects what prices are structurally deaf to, or merely garbles the one message they transmit well.

questions.stem: The passage as a whole is best described as

options.A: a defence of market prices against the charge that they are unfair to those who cannot pay them
options.B: an account of what prices uniquely accomplish, joined to an account of what that same mechanism structurally cannot register
options.C: an argument that central planning fails because planners cannot gather information quickly enough
options.D: a demonstration that interventions in markets garble the only message prices transmit well

explanation.correct_reasoning: P1–p2 establish the achievement (coordination under dispersed knowledge); p3 turns to the built-in limitation ('that discarding ... is also the system's confession'); p4 synthesizes both into a criterion for judging interventions. Option B is the only one that spans this two-sided structure.
explanation.question_type_note: When a passage pivots halfway ('however'), the main idea must include both sides of the pivot; options describing only the first half are too narrow.
explanation.reading_habit: Mark every 'however', 'but', and 'yet' as you read — when a passage pivots, the main idea must hold both halves, and one-sided options are automatic eliminations.

distractors.why_wrong: P1 explicitly sets fairness aside ('never been that they are fair'); the passage does not engage the fairness debate.
distractors.seductive_element: Fairness is the most familiar public controversy about prices.

distractors.why_wrong: The planning critique is one paragraph's supporting point, and the passage's second half concerns price failures, not planner failures.
distractors.seductive_element: P1 genuinely contains this claim, stated forcefully.

distractors.why_wrong: P4 says the question is whether an intervention corrects structural deafness or garbles the signal — explicitly allowing that some interventions do the former.
distractors.seductive_element: It lifts p4's 'garbles' language while dropping its balancing clause.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the "parsimony" of the price system consists in the fact that

options.A: it instructs all users of a good identically, regardless of their trade
options.B: it conveys the combined consequence of many facts in a single figure while omitting the facts themselves
options.C: it operates without any authority intending the coordination it achieves
options.D: it economizes on scarce goods faster than a planner with complete files could

explanation.correct_reasoning: P2's final sentence defines the marvel of parsimony directly: 'a single figure carries the aggregate consequence of countless facts, while discarding the facts themselves.' Option B restates exactly this compression-plus-omission.
explanation.question_type_note: Detail questions are answered by returning to the text and matching the claim, not the vocabulary; the strongest distractors are true statements from nearby sentences attached to the wrong term.
explanation.reading_habit: Put your finger on the exact sentence where a tested term is defined before touching the options — never accept a true statement that lives one sentence away from the term.

distractors.why_wrong: The passage does say every user receives the same instruction, but that uniformity is not what the word parsimony is attached to.
distractors.seductive_element: It is a faithful detail from the same paragraph.

distractors.why_wrong: Unintended coordination is p2's opening observation, not the definition of parsimony given at its close.
distractors.seductive_element: Also true, also from p2 — proximity does the tempting.

distractors.why_wrong: The passage says the adjustments accomplish what a planner 'could scarcely match,' a claim about outcome, not about speed; and this is not the parsimony claim.
distractors.seductive_element: It blends the planner comparison with the economize instruction.

questions.stem: The example of pollution in the third paragraph is used to illustrate situations in which

options.A: a price is distorted because a cartel, rather than genuine scarcity, has raised it
options.B: the costs of an activity are borne by people who never transact in the priced good, so the price omits them
options.C: buyers respond to a price without knowing the reason for its level
options.D: an intervention has garbled the message that the price would otherwise transmit

explanation.correct_reasoning: P3 offers two failure cases: the cartel (misleading 'why') and costs that 'fall on people who never transact in it,' where the price 'falls silent.' Pollution — 'no invoice,' lungs darkened by coal — is given as the instance of the second case.
explanation.question_type_note: When a paragraph lists two failure modes and then gives one example, the question tests which mode the example instantiates — read the sentence structure, not just the topic.
explanation.reading_habit: Whenever an example appears after a list of cases, pause one beat and attach it to its exact case — examples belong to specific claims, not to paragraphs in general.

distractors.why_wrong: The cartel is the paragraph's other failure case, explicitly contrasted with the externality case that pollution illustrates.
distractors.seductive_element: It comes from the very same sentence cluster.

distractors.why_wrong: Responding without knowing the reason is p2's description of the system working well, not of its failure.
distractors.seductive_element: It is a genuine feature of prices described in the passage.

distractors.why_wrong: No intervention appears in the pollution example; garbling by intervention is a possibility raised only abstractly in p4.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the author's claim in the final paragraph about how interventions should be judged?

options.A: A tax on emissions led firms to cut pollution efficiently, while a freeze on coal prices during a genuine shortage produced hoarding and misallocation.
options.B: Most historical interventions in prices were enacted for political reasons rather than to correct externalities.
options.C: Planners in centrally administered economies consistently failed to anticipate local shortages.
options.D: Consumers rarely know why the price of any good they buy has changed.

explanation.correct_reasoning: P4's criterion predicts a difference in outcomes: interventions correcting structural deafness (an emissions tax pricing the unpriced) should work, while interventions garbling the scarcity signal (a price freeze) should fail. Option A supplies exactly this paired evidence, one case per side of the criterion.
explanation.question_type_note: Strengthen questions reward the option that raises the probability of the specific claim asked about — here a two-sided criterion, best supported by evidence discriminating between its two sides.
explanation.reading_habit: To strengthen a two-sided claim, hunt for evidence that discriminates between the sides — one clean case per side outweighs ten cases piled on one.

distractors.why_wrong: The motives behind past interventions say nothing about whether the author's proposed test correctly sorts good interventions from bad.
distractors.seductive_element: It sounds like hard-nosed realism about intervention.

distractors.why_wrong: This supports p1's knowledge-problem premise, which is not the final paragraph's claim about judging interventions.
distractors.seductive_element: It strengthens something the author does say — in a different paragraph.

distractors.why_wrong: Consumer ignorance of reasons is the mechanism of p2, already assumed; it does not bear on the intervention criterion.
distractors.seductive_element: It echoes the passage's most striking descriptive point.

vocabulary.passage_use: scattered across millions of minds, much of it tacit
vocabulary.meaning_here: understood or possessed without being stated or articulated

vocabulary.passage_use: The marvel is the parsimony of it
vocabulary.meaning_here: extreme economy or sparingness — here, of information

vocabulary.passage_use: a single figure carries the aggregate consequence of countless facts
vocabulary.meaning_here: combined into a total; taken as a sum

mentor.challenge: The passage praises something for two paragraphs and then spends two paragraphs on its limits. The questions live at that hinge — watch for it.
mentor.main_idea: A price is a brilliant messenger that tells everyone how much of a thing to use without telling anyone why. That silence is its power — and exactly why it fails where the 'why' matters, like pollution nobody pays for.
mentor.author_intention: To move the reader past both worship and dismissal of markets, and install a sharper question: does this policy fix what prices cannot hear, or scramble the one thing they say well?

paragraph_journey.note: The knowledge needed for economic decisions is scattered across millions of minds — no committee can gather it in time. This sets the bar prices are about to clear.
A rising price makes everyone economize without knowing why. One number carries the conclusion of countless facts while throwing the facts away.
'That discarding is also the system's confession' — the hinge. Prices carry no reasons, so they mislead under cartels and fall silent on costs nobody pays, like pollution.
Neither worship nor dismissal: judge any intervention by whether it corrects the deafness or garbles the signal.

mentor.tone_progression: Admiring, almost wonder-struck, through paragraph 2; then soberly critical at the hinge; ending balanced and practical — the classic praise-then-limits arc.
key_transitions: 'That discarding, however, is also the system's confession.' (start of paragraph 3) — the entire passage pivots on this sentence.
'The mature conclusion is therefore neither worship nor dismissal.' (start of paragraph 4) — both halves are folded into one criterion.
mentor.misunderstanding: Most misreadings come from stopping at the marvel: two admiring paragraphs convince the hurried reader this is 'a pro-market passage', and paragraph 3's reversal never updates that first impression. A smaller group over-corrects, treats the pollution paragraph as the thesis, and reads it as anti-market. It is neither — it is about what one instrument can and cannot carry, and every question rewards holding both halves at once.
mentor.traps_summary: A two-sided passage breeds one-sided options. Wrong answers here took only the praise half or only the critique half, or moved a true claim to the wrong side of the hinge. When a passage pivots, the right answer almost always honours both sides.
mentor.real_world: Debates about carbon taxes, price caps, and surge pricing are all paragraph 4 in disguise: is the policy pricing something unpriced, or jamming the signal? You now have the question that cuts through them.
mentor.takeaway: A price tells you how much, never why — trust it for the first, never the second.
